dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Transfer/Interfacility/Palliative Care call at Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call EMS today?
I'm having severe shortness of breath due to heart failure.
Can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
My name is Maria, and we are in front of my doctor's office in Richmond.
Do you know what day and time it is?
It's Tuesday, and I believe it's around 1:00 PM.
Are you alert to your surroundings and your condition?
Yes, I'm aware of everything.
Ma'am, we are going to check your breathing. May I listen to your lungs and note your breathing effort?
Pulse oximeter reading is 80. We are going to start high-flow oxygen at 15 liters via non-rebreather mask.
Your breathing rate is around 40 and you're tachypneic with audible rales and cold, clammy skin.
Respirations - 40 breaths per minute.
SpO₂ is 80% on room air; we are now giving you 15L non-rebreather ox.
Pulse is very rapid; objectively measured at 120 BPM.
Blood pressure is 173/86 with strong peripheral pulses.
Blood glucose reading is not possible here with our field equipment.
Airway is currently open and you are responsive. Have you experienced any difficulty speaking or breathing more so now?
Let me place you on a 4-lead EKG monitor. Please stay still.
Glasgow Coma Scale reveals your full awareness-GCS 15 (eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6).
Ma'am, any chest pain or other symptoms today?
No chest pain, but I am having very severe shortness of breath and auscultated rales.
I will note in documentation that you are reporting no chest pain.
I see on record you have a history of end-stage heart failure and a pacemaker.
Yes, I have had chronic heart failure and a pacemaker for several years.
You mentioned you were enroute to an appointment when this started. What were you doing before symptoms appeared?
I was in the car, sitting still. Suddenly had severe dyspnea but no chest discomfort.
I need to gauge the severity of your breathing. On a scale of 0-10, how severe is it now?
A 10. I can't take a normal breath. Must stay upright.
I'm going to assess your skin for now. I note that you are cold and clammy. Any rashes or lesions?
No. I don't have any rashes or lesions. Just this severe dyspnea from heart failure.
Can you tell me today's date again?
Yes, it's the 8th of January.
What county are we in right now?
Henrico County, I think.
And what happened that led you to call EMS today?
I was driving, fine at first, then I began having sudden and severe shortness of breath.
I need to reconfirm you are showing alertness and awareness. If I ask you to squeeze my hand, can you do so?
Of course, do it.
Thank you. You remain alert and responsive.
Your current medications include torsemide, Pepcid, and Lipase?
Yes, that's correct.
Next, I am going to begin IV insertion and a 12-lead EKG with your consent.
Attempting IV access in your arm and leg showed three failed attempts. This is now logged.
We are now placing you on a 12-lead EKG for the cardiology team at the hospital.
Resp is down to 28 breaths per minute with oxygen at 15L NRB.
SpO₂ has improved to 100%.
Pulse is lower now at 113 BPM, likely due to oxygen support.
Blood pressure remains at 164/97 with consistent pulse quality.
12-lead EKG reveals atrial flutter with left bundle branch block pattern noted.
I confirm that we are following protocol 2-3 for pulmonary edema and CHF.
Rechecking vital signs now-pulse, respirations, and BP.
We are maintaining your current interventions of high-flow oxygen and EKG monitoring.
Let me reconfirm your SOB severity. Still a 10 out of 10?
Yes, it's unrelenting. I still must remain upright.
Are you experiencing any new symptoms now?
Still severe dyspnea. No chest pain.
Confirming oxygen and EKG are functioning and EKG waveform is normal other than reported atrial flutter.
SpO₂ remains at 100% on 15L NRB, and breathing rate is now 28.
IV access remained unsuccessful, but no signs of phlebitis or infiltration.
No medications were given, and interventions are fully documented.
EMS team agrees to continue with protocol 2-3 for this case.
All vitals remain unchanged. SpO₂ 100%, BP 164/97, RR 28.
We're now preparing you for removal from the car to the EMS cot. We will be gentle and monitor you closely. Are you ready?
Do what you must. I'm ready, but I may not want to live.
We are aware you have a DNR. We respect your wishes and will facilitate transport with comfort in mind.
You are now on the EMS cot, and all restraints are in place. I'll continue to monitor your vitals.
Oxygen is at 15L and vitals are stable. We are five minutes from the hospital. Transport will begin now.
Checking your airway once again. It is clear and non-stridorous. No neck swelling.
Quick assessment of your lungs again. Rales still audible.
In five-minute warning from the hospital. I am repeating your vitals now.
Resp remains at 28 and SpO₂ is 100%.
Blood pressure is still at 164/97 on current monitoring.
All vitals are normal and no change in your condition has been observed.
Secondary assessment repeated and no issues reported. All stable.
Reconfirmed that you remain alert and oriented. No new symptoms reported.
Hospital is now able to accept patients. We are ready to arrive at their facility.
Bedside report has been sent, and a bed is being prepared at the hospital. Arrival expected within 10 minutes.
You have been moved on the EMS cot as documented. All restraints and positioning secure for transport.
Reassurance: No pain has been reported, consistent with field record, and you remain a "0" for pain as in the ePCR.
